title: 10 Top-Rated Herbal Creams for the Treatment of Peripheral Arterial Occlusive Disease

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba cream is a topical treatment that may be beneficial for people with peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD). This condition occurs when the blood vessels in the legs become narrowed or blocked, reducing blood flow to the muscles and skin. Ginkgo biloba cream contains a plant extract that helps improve blood flow and oxygen delivery to the affected areas. It works by increasing the production of a protein called nitric oxide, which causes blood vessels to relax and widen. This allows more blood to flow through the narrowed vessels, helping to reduce symptoms such as pain, cramping, and discoloration in the legs. Regular use of Ginkgo biloba cream may also help reduce inflammation and promote healing in the affected tissues. Additionally, it may improve the overall function of the blood vessels, allowing for better oxygen delivery and waste removal. The increased blood flow and oxygen delivery can help to promote healing and reduce symptoms of PAOD, making Ginkgo biloba cream a potential treatment option for people with this condition.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hamamelis virginiana herbal creams that help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, start by infusing the dried Witch Hazel (Hamamelis virginiana) leaves and bark in a carrier oil like sweet almond oil or coconut oil. Combine 1 part of dried Witch Hazel with 2-3 parts of carrier oil in a clean glass jar, and let it sit for 2-3 weeks in a cool, dark place, shaking the jar occasionally. After the infusion is complete, strain the oil through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. Next, melt 1 part of beeswax and 1 part of cocoa butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water. Once the wax and cocoa butter are fully melted, slowly add the infused oil to the melted wax and cocoa butter, stirring constantly until the mixture is smooth and well combined. Remove the mixture from the heat and let it cool until it reaches a consistency that can be easily poured into small jars or containers. Once cooled, the Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream is ready to use, and can be applied topically to affected areas 2-3 times a day to help improve blood flow and alleviate symptoms of peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedies.image_alt: Ginkgo biloba herbal creams for peripheral arterial occlusive disease

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hamamelis virginiana herbal creams that help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, first gather the necessary ingredients: dried Hamamelis virginiana leaves, a carrier oil such as coconut oil, beeswax, and a preservative like vitamin E oil. Start by making a herbal infusion by steeping one part of dried Hamamelis virginiana leaves in two parts of boiling water for 10-15 minutes, then straining the mixture and letting it cool. Next, melt the carrier oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water, then stir in the cooled herbal infusion and the vitamin E oil. Continue heating and stirring until the mixture reaches a temperature of 160-180°F (71-82°C), which will help kill off any bacteria and preserve the cream. Pour the mixture into a clean container and let it cool before transferring it to a storage container. Once set, the Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream can be applied topically to the affected area, helping to reduce inflammation and improve circulation in people with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

remedies.remedy_desc: Panax ginseng cream is used to help people with peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD). remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hamamelis virginiana herbal creams that help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, you will need to start by harvesting the leaves and twigs of the Hamamelis virginiana plant, also known as witch hazel. The leaves and twigs should be dried thoroughly, either by air-drying or using a dehydrator, to preserve their medicinal properties. Once the plant material is dry, it should be crushed or ground into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle. This powder can then be mixed with a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, in a ratio of about 1 part witch hazel powder to 10 parts carrier oil. The mixture should be heated gently, either in a double boiler or in the microwave, until the powder has fully incorporated into the oil. The mixture should then be cooled and strained to remove any solids, and finally, it can be whipped or blended with a small amount of beeswax to thicken it and create a cream. This Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream can be applied topically to the affected area, helping to reduce inflammation and improve blood flow to the skin, which can be beneficial for individuals with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia creams, also known as lavender oil creams, contain compounds that help improve blood flow to the legs. remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream that helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, also known as poor circulation, you'll need to prepare the herbal extract first. Start by harvesting fresh Hamamelis virginiana leaves, which have natural astringent and anti-inflammatory properties that can help improve blood flow and reduce swelling. Dry the leaves in a warm, dark place or using a food dehydrator until they're completely dry and crumbly. Then, combine the dried leaves with a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, in a ratio of 1 part herb to 2 parts oil. Steep the mixture in a clean glass jar in a warm, dark place for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every few days, to allow the herb's active compounds to infuse into the oil. After the infusion period, strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container, discarding the solids. Next, mix the herbal extract with a thickening agent, such as beeswax or shea butter, in a ratio of 1 part extract to 1 part thickening agent, and heat the mixture gently in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl until the beeswax or shea butter is fully melted. Finally, pour the mixture into a clean glass jar or tin and let it cool and solidify, creating a rich and nourishing Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream that can be applied topically to help improve blood flow and reduce swelling in affected areas.

remedies.remedy_desc: Centella asiatica cream is a great option for people with peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD). This condition occurs when the blood vessels in the legs become narrow and block the flow of blood, causing pain and fatigue. Centella asiatica cream helps to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation. remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream that may help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, start by making a herbal infusion. First, dry the leaves of the Hamamelis virginiana plant, also known as witch hazel, by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down in a warm, dry place. Once dry, chop the leaves into small pieces and place one tablespoon of the chopped leaves in a clean glass jar. Add one cup of boiling water to the jar, cover it, and let it steep for 10-15 minutes. Strain the liquid and discard the leaves. Combine the liquid with one cup of a carrier oil, such as olive or coconut oil, in a separate bowl. Let the mixture sit at room temperature for 2-3 weeks, shaking the bowl occasionally, until the liquid is fully incorporated into the oil. This is called maceration. After the maceration process, add a thickening agent, such as beeswax or candelilla wax, to the mixture to thicken it into a creamy consistency. Melt the wax in a double boiler or in the microwave, then slowly add it to the mixture, stirring constantly until it reaches the desired consistency. Finally, add a few drops of a preservative, such as vitamin E oil, to prevent the cream from spoiling. Transfer the cream to a clean container and store it in the refrigerator to help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease symptoms, such as pain and swelling in the legs.

remedies.remedy_desc: Salvia miltiorrhiza cream is a treatment for peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD), a condition where narrowed or blocked arteries reduce blood flow to the legs. remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hamamelis virginiana herbal creams that help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, start by harvesting the leaves and twigs of the Witch Hazel plant (Hamamelis virginiana) in late summer or early fall. Clean and dry the plant material, then chop it into small pieces and add it to a large pot of cold water. Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes to extract the active compounds. Next, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container, discarding the solids. Let the liquid cool, then combine it with a carrier oil such as coconut or olive oil in a ratio of 1:3 (one part Hamamelis virginiana extract to three parts carrier oil). Mix in beeswax and a preservative of your choice, then heat the mixture gently until the beeswax has melted. Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and solidify. Once set, label and store the Hamamelis virginiana herbal cream in a cool, dry place. This cream can be applied topically to the affected area to help reduce inflammation, promote blood flow, and alleviate symptoms of peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

remedies.remedy_desc: Vitis vinifera cream, made from the grape vine plant, is being researched for its potential benefits in treating peripheral arterial occlusive disease. This condition occurs when the blood vessels in the legs become narrowed or blocked, causing pain and limited mobility. remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hamamelis virginiana herbal creams that may help with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, start by gathering the necessary ingredients. You will need 1 part of Hamamelis virginiana leaves, flowers, and twigs (also known as witch hazel), 1 part of a carrier oil such as sweet almond oil or coconut oil, and 1/2 part of beeswax. You can either grow or purchase the Hamamelis virginiana plant, but make sure to identify it correctly and only use the leaves, flowers, and twigs, as the roots contain toxic compounds.  Next, dry the Hamamelis virginiana plant parts in a cool and dark place to preserve their properties. You can do this by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down or by spreading them out on a paper bag. Once the plant parts are completely dry, you can make the herbal infusion by steeping 1 part of the dried plant in 2 parts of hot water for about 10 minutes, then straining the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter. This infusion is the medicinal liquid that will be used to create the cream.  To make the cream, combine the infused liquid with the carrier oil in a saucepan and heat the mixture over low heat until it reaches a temperature of about 160°F (71°C). Remove the saucepan from the heat and stir in the beeswax until it is fully melted and the mixture has cooled down to around 120°F (49°C). Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and solidify. Once the cream is solid, you can apply it to the affected areas to help improve circulation and reduce inflammation. Always do a patch test before using the cream on a larger area, as some people may be sensitive to the ingredients.

intro_desc: Peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD) occurs when the blood vessels in the legs become narrowed or blocked, causing pain, cramping, and limited mobility. Using herbal creams can help alleviate symptoms and improve blood flow to the affected areas. Ginkgo biloba is an effective herb in PAOD treatment due to its ability to improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation. It contains flavonoids and terpenoids that help expand blood vessels and promote the growth of new ones. On the other hand, Zingiber officinale (ginger) has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce swelling and pain in the affected areas. Its active compounds, gingerols and shogaols, also improve blood flow and reduce oxidative stress. Fixing PAOD is essential because it can lead to more severe complications, such as gangrene or amputation, if left untreated. By addressing the condition, individuals can regain their independence and improve their quality of life. They can walk longer distances, participate in physical activities, and enjoy daily tasks without pain and discomfort. Using herbal creams containing Ginkgo biloba and Zingiber officinale can be a natural and non-invasive way to manage PAOD symptoms and improve overall well-being.
supplementary_best: For peripheral arterial occlusive disease, a combination of 'Ginkgo biloba', 'Salvia miltiorrhiza', and 'Crataegus monogyna' herbal creams can be beneficial. Ginkgo biloba improves blood flow to the limbs by dilating blood vessels and reducing inflammation. Salvia miltiorrhiza, also known as Chinese sage, enhances blood flow and helps reduce blood pressure, which can alleviate symptoms of peripheral arterial occlusive disease. Crataegus monogyna, or hawthorn, strengthens the heart and improves blood circulation, which helps to counteract the effects of the disease. The combination of these three herbs works well together because they address multiple aspects of the disease, including blood flow, inflammation, and heart function. This combination can be effective in reducing symptoms and improving quality of life for people with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
supplementary_avoid: If you have peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD), it's essential to avoid certain herbal creams that can worsen symptoms. One such herb is Coleus forskohlii, which can lower blood pressure too much. This is a problem because people with PAOD already have narrowed blood vessels, and low blood pressure can further reduce blood flow to the limbs. Another herb to avoid is Epimedium sagittatum, which can also lower blood pressure and affect blood vessel function.  Foeniculum vulgare, or fennel, may seem harmless, but it can cause blood vessels to constrict, reducing blood flow to the limbs. This is particularly concerning for people with PAOD, who already have narrowed blood vessels.  Additionally, Astragalus membranaceus can slow down the heart rate, reducing blood flow to the limbs. This can worsen symptoms of PAOD.  Finally, Tribulus terrestris can increase blood pressure, but in people with PAOD, this can lead to increased strain on the already narrowed blood vessels, further reducing blood flow to the limbs.  It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal creams, especially if you have PAOD.
supplementary_other_remedies: People with peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAD) may benefit from natural remedies like omega-3 fatty acid supplements. These supplements help reduce inflammation in the body, which is a major contributor to PAD. Inflammation causes the blood vessels to become narrowed and hardened, making it harder for blood to flow to the legs. Omega-3 fatty acids, found in fish oil, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help slow down this process.  Another natural remedy is turmeric, which contains a compound called curcumin. Curcumin has strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help protect the blood vessels from damage. It also has a compound called nitric oxide, which helps to relax and widen the blood vessels, making it easier for blood to flow. Some people with PAD also find relief from regular exercise, such as walking or cycling, which helps to improve blood flow and strengthen the muscles.  Dietary changes, such as eating foods rich in antioxidants like berries, leafy greens, and other fruits and vegetables, can also help to reduce inflammation and improve blood flow. Smoking cessation is also a natural remedy, as smoking is a major risk factor for PAD. Quitting smoking can significantly improve blood flow and reduce the risk of complications.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with peripheral arterial occlusive disease (PAOD) may also experience other health issues due to reduced blood flow to their legs. These problems can be related to the disease itself or its complications. Some common ailments include:  Numbness, tingling, or pain in the legs and feet due to reduced blood flow. This can be treated with medicinal herbs like Ginkgo biloba, which improves blood circulation and reduces inflammation. Ginkgo biloba's flavonoids and terpenoids help relax blood vessels and improve oxygen delivery to tissues.  Poor wound healing and slow recovery from injuries or surgeries due to inadequate blood supply. The herb Triphala, a blend of three Ayurvedic plants (Amalaki, Bibhitaki, and Haritaki), can aid in wound healing by promoting collagen synthesis and improving blood circulation. Triphala's antioxidant properties also help protect against tissue damage.  Increased risk of infections, such as ulcers or cellulitis, due to impaired blood flow. The herb Echinacea can help boost the immune system and fight off infections. Its compounds, like alkylamides and glycoproteins, stimulate the production of white blood cells and activate the body's natural defense mechanisms.  Muscle weakness and fatigue due to reduced oxygen delivery to muscles. The herb Ashwagandha can help reduce muscle fatigue and improve exercise performance by increasing blood flow and oxygen delivery to muscles. Ashwagandha's adaptogenic properties also help the body adapt to stress and reduce inflammation.  Varicose veins, spider veins, or other circulatory problems due to increased pressure on blood vessels. The herb Butcher's Broom (Ruscus aculeatus) can help reduce inflammation and improve blood vessel function. Its saponins and flavonoids constrict blood vessels, reduce swelling, and improve circulation.  These medicinal herbs can help alleviate symptoms and improve quality of life for individuals with PAOD.
